So, I want to start off with a little experience I had, a little
narrative about an experience. I walk into a room where there are
some people waiting for me to pitch. I open the door, and I stride
in, and I know who's gonna be in there. Three guys worth $100
million. Two billionaires and their three analysts. And what's
interesting in this room, is I know that the three analysts are
worth more than $10 million each. So when I say high stakes, high
pressure environment, learn this stuff in the crucible. So I walk
into the room, stride through, and I open my arms like this and I
look around and I say, “let me decide who I'm gonna kick off the
island first.”
And the one billionaire says, “call security and get this guy the _
outta here.” He didn't say that. So, they laugh, and they came into
my frame. I can do that, I don't recommend you do that on day
one. I can do these things and I'm saying these things, in this way
so you can understand the structure, but you don't have to be as
aggressive as me. I'm aggressive because I've dealt with so many
investors, and investors want economic concessions, for their
power. They want to pay the least, and get the most. So investors
are a little different from customers. This is why I'm so aggressive.
You walk in, and it's kill or be killed. They want economic
concessions. You might hear about venture capitalists, they're
called vulture capitalists. It's the business. When they invest, eight
out of ten deals die, and they have to live on the two that work.
They go in and they want the most amount of your stock, at the
least amount of their money, at the worst terms for you, the best
terms for them, and lock you in for the longest period of time.
This is why I've learned to be aggressive and social. But, animals
and humans that are aggressive physically, and verbally. don't last
long in the modern world. So, I've learned to be aggressive about
frame control and I get it very quickly, but I give it back. It's not
about being dominant, it's about being able to get frame control
to qualify yourself as a prize, to deal with the power frame. But
once you get it, it doesn't mean you keep it, and you dominate,
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	1
Oren Klaff:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 13 --

and you be the alpha, and you beat your chest, and you declare
that you're the winner. You give it back, you play.
I want to say two things quickly. If anyone has ever been to see
Improv comedy, show of hands? OK, this is great stuff. Let me tell
you what's going on there because I've been to some of these
sessions in Hollywood. They can't be that funny all the time, what
they do is they find a game to play, and once they find that game
they play it. That's what you want to do with frame control, you
want to get frame control but then you want to get in a game,
these are social games. In fact, psychologists and psychiatrists
call it game theory. How human beings interact, it's called game
theory. Now don't go type that on the Internet and read it, it's
horrible stuff to read. It's not interesting, it's very academic. But
it's game theory because it's a game. So you want to find a game
that people will play with you, you get frame control but you give
it back.
So for example, and I wrote about this and I've spoken about it
before, but this is the most fun one. My partner, he's a very
interesting guy, and he'll say, “I'll meet you at the office at 3:17,”
and I'll have 19 minutes, I've heard this before. So what I used to
do is get there at 3:17 and be like, oh my god I've only got 19
minutes to do all the work. But then I learned to say, “oh you have
19 minutes that's funny because I only have 18.” But then he'll
say, “OK well then I forgot, I only have 15.” Then I'll say, “that's
funny because you reminded me that I forgot. I have about nine
minutes today, John, I'm sorry. 3:17, I can't even do that math, to
3:25 maybe, 3:24.” And he'll say, OK well I'll meet you there but
we'll just talk from our cars in the parking lot, because I'm super
busy today. So you want to get in a game, and we do this all the
time. And what's really fun is when you learn frame control and
the guy you work with all the time knows frame control, and then
you can play this game. You're working time frames, and power
frames, and you'll say, “oh my God you just worked an analyst
frame stacked on a prize frame with an intrigue frame on top of
it.” It's like these Kung Fu movies! Ah! Crane style! Scorpion!
Elephant! So you learn this game of framing back and forth and
it's fun.
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 13 --

You're gonna find your own way to do this, you don't have to do it
aggressive, as I do it. I've been doing it for many years, and its the
main thing I think about from the waking moments in the day to
the last moments as the sun goes down. It's how can I walk in a
room, get frame control for myself, get all these people under
control, get them wanting what I have, but having fun at the same
time? Do it in a short period of time and have people leaving and
going, “whew, that was awesome!:” I presented to a board of a
semiconductor company, much of the same things that I'm
talking to you about, but in terms of their product. After half an
hour of describing it to them, they stood up and clapped.
Now that's interesting because it's a board meeting, and that's
not typical behavior that you would stand up and clap. I'm not
entertainment! I've never experienced that before, but now,
because I'm getting stronger and I really care about frame control,
not just getting it but giving it, and finding a game, and letting
people know I'm the prize and they're the commodity. But letting
them play with that and have fun, and letting people know I care
more about having fun than I care about money. So now I'm
hearing things like, “who are you?” Heard that a couple times.
Give a pitch, who are you? “Where did you come from?” I came
from Mars to pitch the Earth. But when you get good at doing this
it is amazing. In some ways it's unfortunate the word, control, in
frame control. Because you can think it's about dominating or
selling or overcoming rejection or getting. But it's really about
giving. Imagine that, you're giving someone a game to play in a
business meeting that's fun, in which they can get value for
themselves. It's awesome and you'll get acknowledged for it.
I want to say a couple little things. Sometimes I imagine when I
meet someone, there is a pipe between us, and value flows
between this pipe. Because human exchanges are value
exchanges. In our house, in your house probably the same, you
cook, other person does the dishes. You wash the cars, they walk
the dog. I don't know what happens in your house, but it should
be something like that, otherwise I don't want to come over. It's
social reciprocation. So we try to extend that to business
meetings. We'll say, “OK, I give you a little bit of information. Now
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 13 --

you give me some interest, or you give me some value.” We think
it's a reciprocal value exchange, but it's not.
It doesn't work that way in a business context. We have to give
Huge amounts of value and that's what frame control is good for.
So if there's a translucent pipe between Pam and I, it's an awfully
long pipe today, all I'm trying to do is shovel value into this pipe,
as much and as fast as possible. I'm not selling, I'm looking to
deliver value. I'm not asking her questions, because questions
suck value. Whenever you find yourself asking a question to
someone, you are sucking value back through that value pipe,
you're not adding value into it. So shovel value. You know, the
blue plastic frogs, describe how they're made and why you should
care and what kind semiconductor they put up their rear to make
out the front come insect repelling rays. And what's happening to
oil and why it's fun to go to France and you should go there and
see these things in action and value, value, value, value. And I
don't want to ask questions unless it's an imperative. Give value,
and eventually what will happen, the human condition. We do
understand there's reciprocal relationships, because in her house
and in your house you do. You know, I go get the mail, and the
other person pays the bills. There are these reciprocal
relationships, but when you meet someone, they're just loaded
differently. So add tons of value, and eventually that reciprocal
nature will kick in. And she'll go, oh I've gotta give some value
back, and she'll start contributing. And no questions were asked.
So another way you know you have frame control is when you
make a statement, and somebody falls into that statement and
starts to engage with you.
Questions force people to engage with you. I hear a lot of sales
trainers say, “ask questions to get information.” Do you think the
person you're selling to is an idiot? They know you're asking
questions to get information so you can get information, so you
can leverage that information, and sell them. Questions take
value. So don't just stop asking questions because your business
is going to go to zero and people will get mad at me, but just
observe what happens when people ask you questions, how you
feel inside, and when you ask other people questions, what it
does to the social environment. You'll see statements that people
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 13 --

join into create these amazing social exchanges. Questions are
just forcing someone to interact with you. These are just some
general things about the subject.
Where I think I should go next, so people don't start saying, Oren,
you're an amazing theoratician, you should be a college professor,
or write a book or something. Seriously, my dad was a college
professor and I have deep respect for that profession. He was an
academic, he was a demographer. When I got asked to write a
book, initially my reaction was, I'm in business. I do investment
banking. I am in the middle of large deals. I am flying to Geneva
and Russia and to La Jolla. I'm doing deals, why would I ever want
to mess with this? But then, something in my background and
upbringing kicked in, and I got into it and it was a great
experience, I'd love to be able to share that stuff. What I do want
to to is make this deeply felt, this material, and let you know it's
real world too. So I thought I would give you a pitch of a live deal
that I've raised $15 million for, in the last two months. And some
of this money I raised from people who don't speak English. To me
that's meaningful, it's one of the first times I've done that.
So you've got a pitch you're going to hear in a moment on
something very complicated, genetics. Molecular diagnostics.
Barely understand what the name of it is, go pitch it to somebody
in a different country who doesn't speak English, have them write
you a $5 million check, then you know you're the real deal. I'm
flying home from Moscow, and I'm just shaking my head saying, “I
cannot believe that was possible.” So I want to give you a live
pitch, so you can see the exact same thing that I've been out
raising money with. Then I'm going to deconstruct it a bit and
show you the elements of it. I'm gonna shake the Etch-a-Sketch.
So I want to tell you about something very interesting, and thank
you for coming today. If you can just hold off telling me about
yourselves, I'd like to tell you about our genetics company, and
then in context of our company I'd like to hear your firm, and
what kind of things you've done. I know you did great things in the
seventies, and your firm did great things in the eighties, and in
2000 you invested in those three healthcare companies that went
public. That's all very exciting, but this is very topical. As you
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 13 --

know, in any stock presentation they have to disclose that past
performance is not indicative of future performance. So, I don't
think it's a great idea for you guys to tell me all the great things
you've been doing, because it doesn't indicate what you will be
doing. So, let me tell you about my deal, and then you reflect back
to me, about your firm in context of what we have. I can pitch this
in a few minutes, we can go into some Q&A, we have a little less
than an hour to be here, and then unfortunately we have to leave
to be at our next meeting.
So let's try and maximize our time together and then at the end
we can decide if we want to advance to the next step, but you will
be interested in it. It is the dawn of molecular diagnostics. What's
interesting about this, is molecular diagnostics is all about genetic
mutation. So as I look over there, I see he is dark skinned and
medium build. And over there I see someone of fair skin and
slender build and much more height. And over here I see beautiful
women who are both fair skinned and dark haired, and I see
blonde women who have a dark complexion. All this difference
between us is good. What it is is it's genetic mutation, these
differences between us are genetic mutation. And we would not
be able to survive as a species, as a human race, if there weren't
these strong differences, because as we intermix our genes, we
have healthy gene pools to mix from. So mutation makes the
difference between us, and although the word mutation you're
not used to, mutation is good.
Except for when genetic mutation moves two standard deviations
out from the mean. When you have too much mutation, then you
get adverse health conditions and disease, and serious stuff like
diabetes and lung cancer, and cancer of the eyeball, if there is
such a thing. You get serious stuff, you get fibromyalgia and cystic
fibrosis, and very serious health conditions when genetic
mutation moves out too far. Unhealthy. Well today there's a
technology that's changing everything! This technology is
molecular diagnostics, it's the ability to measure markers in the
human DNA. And some markers indicate adverse mutation, and
you can know what disease you're at risk for. So for example
many of you may have heard of the BRCA genetic test that
women can take, and you should take it. So the BRCA test has a
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	6
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 13 --

very high predictive rate of breast cancer. And in fact you'll see
people who test positive for certain parts of the BRCA marker, will
get mastectomies. Without ANY existing disease condition, other
than the genetic marker for that disease, they'll take action now.
It's so predictive of that disease. So genetic markers are predictive
of disease conditions. So your ability to measure an individual
genetic marker, and know what disease it indicates, is an
incredible technology and it's changing the entire health care
system.
In fact, if you haven't heard of this today, I'll tell you that there are
almost 200 drugs with FDA black box labels on it, that say you
should get the genetic companion test for this drug, because the
genetic companion test will tell you if you will be able to
metabolize that drug. Over half of drug spending is wasted,
because you don't have the genetics to metabolize. And you know
this, when you go out drinking and someone says, “oh I can only
drink one drink, I get crazy.” And you have other people that say,
“oh I can drink 50 of these and it doesn't effect me.” That's
genetics. You can't train yourself to drink. Your genetics effect
how you metabolize drugs, and what diseases you have the
propensity to get. So it's very interesting.
If you have a car accident, and you get back pain and you go to
your doctor. He'll start saying, I've got to start cycling you on
oxycontin and morphine and codeine, and there are 238 different
opiates, and we've got to find what you can tolerate. It's
miserable. What does that mean? It means, which of those drugs
for you, you can metabolize and absorb and it takes away the pain
but doesn't turn you into a zombie of the apocolypse. You've got
to find which of those drugs you can metabolize because of your
genetics. So genetic test will tell you what drugs you can
metabolize, what drugs you'll have and adverse health reaction
to, and what diseases could be on your genetic train tracks. So I
want to tell you a little about our company in a second, but let me
summarize the value proposition of a genetic test.
One, it will tell you what disease you are at risk for. Number two,
once you discover which diseases you are likely to get, it will tell
you what preventative measures will be most effective in avoiding
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 13 --

that disease condition. Number three, if you already do have
symptoms of a disease condition, what drugs, diets, and
treatments are most likely to be effective. If there's a better value
proposition than that in healthcare today, I don't know what it is.
This is a massive industry that's growing very very quickly. Now,
let me tell you about our company. Many of you will have taken a
genetic test already. So if you doing an IVF treatment, or if
somebody else in your family is, they will do a genetic test to see
if you have the recessive gene for cystic fibrosis, for lung cancer,
for many different conditions that are recessive conditions you
can pass on. If you're getting pain medication, it's likely that you'll
take a genetic test to see if you can metabolize it. If you're taking
a drug called Warfarin or Plavix, you'll take a genetic test to see if
you're a metabolizer of it. But these genetic tests are done one at
a time, they're called single trait tests.
The test for cystic fibrosis to see if you carry that recessive gene,
is $800 for two people. The test for breast cancer is $3000. The
test for Plavix is $400. The test for lung cancer is $600. The test
for diabetes, for how you metabolize carbohydrates and proteins
and fats, is $1,200. If you took every test that's available, you
could spend $500,000. Our company is called Acme Genomics
trying to avoid giving you the real name. Acme Genomics has
realized that the cost of doing a single genetic test is about the
same as testing for 33,000 genetic markers. So we put it all on a
single chip. We test for 33,000 genetic markers on a single chip
across 1,000 health conditions, diseases, drug interactions, food
metabolization traits. So for $350, a company can buy for their
employees, a genetic test that includes over $150,000 of the cost
of it would be in single trait tests, on a single chip. And from that
you can learn all about your inherited disease, what diseases you
might pass on, what complex diseases are on your genetic train
tracks, how you can prevent those diseases, how you metabolize
food, and what exercises benefit you the most. Across 1,000 of
these conditions for $350.
It can be life changing. The company has tested 350 of the Biggest
Losers. Gatorade and Pepsi have contracted to test 2,000 of their
performance athletes with this test. So that's the value
proposition of a genetic test. Let me tell you a little bit about the
Deconstruction

www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 13 --

company. The company did $10 million last year. They'll do $35
million this year. But unlike many companies, the $35 million that
we'll do this year are under contract. Existing contracts with large
companies you've heard about, that are in the Fortune 50. And by
the way those contracts for 2013 are worth more than $100
million of revenue. So we're here today, raising a $20 million
round of equity for the company at a $200 million valuation.
We've talked to JP Morgan about going public in the beginning of
2014, and JP Morgan does 50% of all healthcare offerings. The
company has 24 resellers across the world, ten of the Fortune
500 are its customers and under contract, and it is the dominant
product in this market. You have the financials in front of you, so
as you can see, with this kind of pace of growth, with these kinds
of customer contracts, with this kind of value proposition, in
which there are no competitors have a product that is within two
years of this one, capital raising is not a problem for the company.
What we're looking for is good partners. People who can promote
the company, find new customers, bring it into new markets. And
so what I'd like to hear from you now, beyond any kind of money
you can offer, because we're going to raise the money in the next
60 days. What I'd like to hear from you is what kind of partner you
can be for the company, what you can bring beyond your money,
and how you could help the company reach its goals and
objectives and its mission. And most importantly, to get an exit
strategy for all of the investors in 2014 with the IPO as we talked
about. So I'll turn the table over to you for a bit, and hear about
your firm in context of our deal. So that is a pitch guys!
That is a pitch that has raised money. As you can see I didn't really
talk that much about most of the things that you would probably
talk about. Very, very little about the product. Because investors
and buyers are much smarter than you think. Once they
understand the value proposition, you don't have to spend
anymore time on your product, they get it. What you have to
spend time on is calibrating how you guys are a fit for each other,
and getting control of the frame.
What I'd like to do is a little bit of a tear down, on this deal so you
can see what happened. Even from the first page of the deck, and
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 13 --

this is something people would see before they even heard the
pitch. From the first page we try and make it novel and
provocative and interesting. This already doesn't look like two
guys and a dog are promoting it. This is very important. In every
pitch, you have to orient people to your deal by telling them
exactly where it is, how many employees you have, what your
product is, who you're for, who you're not for, you have to give
enough information so at the end of your pitch they don't scratch
their head and go, that sounds awesome but what is it again? You
have to orient people to what you have, you have to orient people
to your deal.
I may have to read this to you because the print is really small.
Most people are trained to give product overviews, or brochures,
or executive summaries, or business plans, or Powerpoint
presentations, and all of those are the delivery of information. But
what I have learned to do is give people the big idea, which is a
little bit of everything. I'll read this to you. Today, a single new
technology is changing the economics of every healthcare
segment, genetic diagnostics. Genetic diagnostics is valuable for
three reasons. First, it can predict your chance of getting a serious
disease, including cancer, diabetes, or a heart condition. Second,
it will help you prevent the disease you are at risk for. Third, it will
help you treat any disease condition that you may already have.
How? A genetic test panel will indicate how you metabolize
certain drugs, which is critical information for treating any disease
condition. Today, doctors and patients need genetic tests to make
major healthcare decisions. The company sells large corporate
customers an comprehensive genetic test panel for about $350, a
fraction of the cost of equivalent test results. The genetic
diagnostics tests for 33,000 markers that proved insight on more
than 1,000 disease risks, drug responses, and health conditions.
That is the big idea. It's so important to give people not what your
product is, not what you are, not what they can get, but the big
idea. Some frame, the big context. If you did this, if you provided
this for people, you could improve lives, save lives, make a billion
dollars. It's a big idea, it's a big idea. So to me, this is incredibly
important in the pitch.
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 13 --

Next, this is very interesting. This says product highlights, but I
don't talk about the product. OK? Sold in volume to large
companies, diagnostic quality DNA and reports, 80% gross
margin. You see the product over there, I'm not describing it at all.
Once you describe the value proposition, they get what you have.
You don't spend the whole time talking about the product. They
get it, they are a lot smarter than you think. Especially investors,
but I bet it's the same for your buyers too. You could do a lot less
explaining about the product than you think. Think about yourself.
I could just tell you, I make a product. So you heard about my blue
plastic frogs, and they're very effective at what they do. But I also
make this great thing, it's called a squirrel nut scratcher. How
much more explaining to I have to do? I could talk a little bit more
about the size and the cost, the delivery time, but people are
much smarter. They get into it once they understand a value
proposition, and you name the product they generally know what
it is. When most people come to pitch me a deal, they spend 45
minutes describing their product, but I don't know anything about
the deal, and I don't know anything about them, and I don't know
anything about the relationship.
I'll give you an example, you see this a lot. Hey guys, good to meet
you. Oh I see up on the wall, is that your son playing baseball? You
know my son plays baseball. That's really cool. Oh, he's in little
league? Yeah little league but when we moved from Georgia. Oh,
lived in Georgia? We used to live in Tallahassee which is close we
used to go to Atlanta. Oh, Atlanta? I love Atlanta. Yeah, the Atlanta
Braves. We used to go to their games- What is this? This is
comparison of geography knowledge. This has nothing to do with
pitching, because it's not creating any tension, it's not creating a
social environment, it's not frame control, it's just not knowing
how to do that stuff. It's not knowing how to get frame control
and hunting and pecking for some source of alignment, and it's
not good, and it wastes time. If you believe as I do, that the
human attention span is 20 minutes, then you would not waste a
second telling people you go to Chicago in the summer and that
you go to Springfield to swim in the lake. It's not helpful.
So we were deconstructing this pitch. Company overview, 2012
revenue, long term licensing revenue, customer pipeline of $500
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 13 --

million, the size of the market. Do you see how different this is? As
a company overview, I'm not telling them the details. They can
intuit the details or the things they already know. Where it is, we
already told them how many employees it has. It's in the
important things. Where revenue will come from, what the
sustainability of that revenue is, how much more revenue there is
behind it. That's what makes a company. You can start to see this
pitch. A financial plan. What's interesting about this financial plan
and you've seen hundreds, I give almost no information. Revenue,
gross margin, cost of goods, expenses, and net income. That's it.
If I see a hundred deals, a hundred of them give me much more
information than I can absorb about their financial plan. A very,
very summarized financial plan. Where is the revenue coming
from? An overview of the customer. Proof points. So what's
interesting here is proof points. We're not just saying it's a great
company and revenue is going to come from somewhere, we're
giving proof points. I do it in this way, but you need in your own
business to give proof points.
A little bit more further on, I'm not sure if this is page 10 or page
11, page 12. It's the first time I talk about the product. And I think if
there's one thing to remember here, people care about the deal,
and they care about the social relationship, and they care about
wanting it, but the detail of the products can come much later.
Value proposition. I think I might just read this because I like it a
lot. In the general population disease and dangerous health
conditions go undiagnosed, causing considerable suffering, and
when disease conditions are properly diagnosed, drug
effectiveness is a further problem. 14 of the top 20 drugs have
limited effectiveness for the general population. Plavix, Warfurin,
Stetinza, Warfervere, will any of these work for you? Maybe not.
Most of the population doesn't have the necessary gene mutation
to allow these drugs to be metabolized.
Value proposition, very, very clear. Great clarity. Little bit more
specifics about the product. How do we know that if I invest in this
deal or I buy this product I didn't just enter a roach motel? How do
I know that this investment gets an exit? So we do an exit analysis
to let people know that once you own this product where you
invest in this company, you can resell it where you're gonna make
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 13 --

your money back where you're going to be able to sell the
company off and get your money back, an exit analysis and a very
clear exit strategy.
So this is how you deliver a pitch. In addition to the spoken pitch I
gave, this is the written pitch. You have seen full cycle how you go
out and raise millions of dollars. You can copy this, we have most
of this stuff on the website, you can copy it word for word. You
can watch the pitch I gave. As long as you prize, get frame control,
give a great value proposition, do it in a short period of time, do it
as clearly as I did it, do it with some intrigue, give a great written
document, you will be raising money or getting bigger buyers or
doing partnerships much faster and of larger size than you would
have thought of before.
Deconstruction
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 13 --
